Hey, it's Andre and Sean. Today we are talking about the value of these letters, the 10,000 foot view. I'm going to hand it over to Andre to give you the overview. And I will play mostly the part of the audience, I think, during this recording where I'll ask questions that I imagine you and the audience are thinking. And then we'll sort of build out this overview and a narrated hypothetical scenario that you can see on the screen as well. So Andre, why don't you give us an overview of what we're looking at here? OK, so if we start on the left, the image on the left-hand side here, we have an entry point. And then we have all of our newsletters. And those newsletters are sent over time. We talk about in the module how to think about the themes of your emails and the frequency and things but outside of that, those dots represent whatever your frequency is, that's the gap between each dot. And there's no end point because you're never going to stop doing the newsletters. The different colors represent themes. And again, this is just more as a visual aid than anything else. It's just the idea that sometimes when it makes sense, you can theme the topics of a newsletter. And then below that, these are the webpages, which is just the idea there is some or all of your newsletters can be repurposed as long form content on your blog or your website that then become feelers and expands your world, your world building efforts on your website. It allows people that are browsing around to find your content and then to interact with it. And the little red piece at the bottom is the call to action, which then points back to a newsletter. I mean, it can be on the page. You can have a two-step opt-in at the bottom of every single blog post or piece of content. Or in our case, we have a link that points to a page. And on our slash subscribe page is a long form, some long form copy that then ends in an opportunity for somebody to add themselves to our email list. So it's a single place on our website for people to add themselves to our email list. And all these little red boxes are links that point to that page that allows people to then enter our world and then the loop is closed. Does it make sense? Yeah, I think it's a beautiful example of a virtuous cycle. So we have an audience who we are sending newsletters to. But newsletters have value for that audience and the value that we create for that audience can be repurposed to attract new members of an audience who can then add themselves to the same system that they become part of. So that's really a classic definition of a virtuous cycle or a flywheel if we want to use Jim Collins language. It becomes something that generates momentum because the thing that we're doing, the activity that we're doing to create value for our audience over time, which is really the definition of a value newsletter, is also the activity that can be repurposed to continually attract more people into our audience. And that's certainly something to think about that this is not a single use activity. So when you think about, okay, I'm going to spend X amount of time per week, per month, creating content for a value newsletter, that creates value for an existing audience and it has this additional thing that it serves additional value it creates, which is by bringing even more people into your world. So it's very elegant in that sense. Do we want to talk about any of the themes here? We can give some examples. I think we gave some examples in the module text itself, but we've done multi-part themed series. We had a four-part series over the summer of 2021 called Courage and Perseverance. We had a two-part series more toward the fall of 2021 where we were looking at creating better prospects. It doesn't, nothing says you have to do themes, but within the larger theme of emergent marketing, which is the theme of our newsletter, there are sub-themes that make sense to express across multiple value newsletters. And it just seems, again, in the way that the colors show, that's not always the case. We're not always within some larger theme, but if you can imagine a multi-part series within the larger series has the effective pulling attention forward. At the bottom of the screen, Andres, I want to draw attention to something and then have you explain this. So when someone enters into our world, our collective, our people listening to this and to you and I, when someone enters our world through a relationship building series, what happens after that? What's the relationship between relationship building series and then the value newsletter? Yeah, so the relationship building series is, as soon as somebody adds themselves to an email list, we can think of them as somebody that maybe doesn't know us. I mean, I think that's a safe assumption. Some people will, let's just say most people won't. So that's an opportunity to bond with that person and create some sort of resonance. They're in the right place. This is what we stand for. This is what we do. And we send this newsletter out every week, every month, whenever we send it out. So we communicate that to them on the last email of our relationship building series and that queues them up so then they know and expect to receive our newsletters. Yeah, so that's kind of the loop over here. So at the bottom of the page here, which is why it's grayed out. It's because it doesn't really, this is not the topic that we're talking about now. That's the relationship building series, but that's what's there grayed out. It's just to represent that the fact that people have to first go through that sequence before they end up on the value user. Yeah, I think that's a critical distinction that you've made clear in the diagram and I wanted to reinforce too, is that when someone comes into our world, and let's just for the sake of our argument, we'll use our emergent marketing newsletter as an example. There's seven parts to that series. But what doesn't happen, someone doesn't come into our world and then and see two parts of the relationship building series and then get our Friday email in the middle of all of that. What we're doing here and showing on the screen instead is that this is a sequence that someone goes through the entirety of the relationship building series. First, that is how we set the stage. We are expressing empathy, beginning to establish credibility and authority, but we're giving people who generally don't know who we are, a sense of who we are, what this is all about, whether or not it's for them. At the end of that, we have prepared them to then receive the value newsletter, which is value over time is really what we're doing. So we've established a relationship initially and with some significant frequency in our case, it's daily. And then we're recognizing that that's not just a box that we check and say, okay, off we go, we've now established a relationship, let's get to selling. That's not the point at all. The point instead is to recognize that the value of maintaining that relationship is significant and the value newsletter becomes the thread that continues to the entirety of the relationship where it's almost a way to think about it as a placeholder that reminds us to, with whatever frequency we've chosen, it reminds us to keep coming back to that idea of maintaining and expanding on that relationship, deepening trust, having opportunities to demonstrate empathy, legitimately demonstrate empathy and develop authority and eventually reach to the level of preeminence over a significant period of time. Do you want to move on to the right now and put this in a larger context with this hypothetical scenario? Yeah, so if we zoom out and again, so this hypothetical scenario represents one of a million different ways that you can rig up your auto email system. So in this example, everybody has to go through a relationship building series, which is a little piece in the green. And in this scenario, there's a soft bridge that then goes to a story-powered promotion. At the end of that story-powered promotion, they're tagged as having completed, at that point, they're either a customer or they're not. And regardless of if they make a purchase and become a customer or not, they're then tagged as now they can receive the value newsletter. In our scenario, on our website, we don't have the soft bridge or the SPP currently. So we go straight from an RBS directly to a VNL and then people will start to receive our value newsletters. In this scenario, there's a promotion that's inserted inside of there, which will fit many scenarios for people that are selling products, they're driving traffic through paid acquisition and they want to recoup or attempt to recoup some of their investment sooner rather than later. That's an opportunity to do that, at which point they then add it to the VNL loop and they can receive the newsletters. So yeah, so those grade dots that represent the VNLs over time, over time, when you zoom out like this, you can see that these newsletters become the spawn of the system over time because things spawn from that, from value newsletters, you can also send out single bridge emails, but for the most part, newsletters become the mechanism in which you dispense information, value to an audience and through that value, people get to self-select out and go off in different directions, they could raise their hand to go into a soap opera series, they can raise their hand for a hard bridge that then goes into another story-part promotion. There's all these different opportunities that can happen, but they all happen as a result of this continued delivery of value over time through the mechanism of the newsletters. And I think the scenario that you've drawn on the right here calls attention to something that's really, really important in the art of email, which is a traditional quote unquote funnel forces the audience to adapt to it. That's just the nature of it, and that's really the metaphor of the funnel is the top is wide, there are lots of people go into the top, you hear all the terms, top of funnel, middle of funnel, et cetera. But by the time you get to the bottom of the funnel, that's where customers kind of come out the bottom, but it's a, that metaphor, it loses so much around how the world actually works. And this, the diagram on the right here with the hypothetical scenario, shows the way in which the art of email creates a system that adapts to the user, rather than forcing the user to adapt to the system. So in a traditional funnel, if you want to get value from that, there's really a, there's a single path that you need to follow. So perhaps you've clicked on an ad, you've gone to a landing page, you've either purchased a low cost offer or you've opted in, there's a series of emails about the thing that you should buy, and you buy or you don't, and then there's probably some number of emails reminding you about why you should buy and all the benefits. There are probably another email that stacks on some benefits. There might be a discount at some point, there might be a payment plan, I mean, all of these things are very predictable, but you can imagine that for me to extract value from that system, I have to follow that system. And what we're showing in the scenario on the right is that the system is responding to the user instead. So somebody might, and these are real examples, sort of representational examples that we've heard over time, is somebody might sign up for our emergent marketing newsletter saying, so and so said that I should know about you guys. So then we get that feedback occasionally to some of our early emails. So someone doesn't know who we are, but a friend says, hey, you should know about this. Well, they're not looking for a particular course, necessarily, they're not, they don't have a specific need. That person might go through a relationship building series, may or may not feel like, resonate with what we're saying. And if they do, what we could, for the sake of argument, some number of people don't have any need for what we're offering other than information for a period of time. So they might see five, six, 10, 15, 30 newsletters over time, but that's keeping us in their awareness. And that awareness is an incredibly valuable asset for us to have, but perhaps we'll use a fictitious example. Again, let's assume that that person who we just talked about signed up has no need whatsoever for anything that we're doing now, other than information and it's valuable. And we've established an initial relationship and we've built on that relationship, say over the course of a year. And again, for the sake of argument, imagine that person now has a need to learn about email marketing, either for his or her own business, perhaps for an employer, so say we really need to figure out how to do our email, but whatever it is, well, in that moment, we have spent the last, if you think about it from zooming out, we spent the last year preparing ourselves to be the best solution to that question, which has now revealed itself for that person. We've built their trust. We didn't ask them, we didn't say on day one, you need to buy this thing or not. We said, here's the value that we can create for you. And when that person's circumstance changes, the system has been perfectly responsive to their journey. And then perhaps they buy the art of email or something else happens at that point. But if you can imagine that layer by layer, that the system is always adapting to the inputs of the people who are going through it, adapting to their behavior, and then showing them what is most relevant to them based on what they're interested in. And you begin to understand the power of the art of email and the thread, the spine, that creates that continual awareness is the value newsletter. That's the thing that's the, almost the metronome week after week, or bi-weekly, monthly, whatever it is, that's the constant that's running across the attention of our audience. And people may not, they may not read every newsletter. They may not engage with everything. But when they want to, the system is responding to them. And that's such a fundamental difference. To me, that's at the core of the art of email and that responsiveness, that ability to have the system adapt to the user's needs in that moment, in their context is profound. Anything on that same theme, Andre, before we start thinking about wrapping up here, any, really just, you go in any direction you wanna go at this point? I think we've laid out the 10,000 foot view of what the value newsletter does. It becomes the spawn of the entire system. And as you said, it's the mechanism for which we remain in people's conscious awareness over time, add value to their lives. It doesn't have to be in every single email. But over time, we're the ones that, or whoever's sending out their news that is, becomes that preeminent piece that if anybody's ever looking for a solution to a problem and you sell a course for product on that thing, I think that's where everything falls into place because it becomes easy for people to make that decision. Yeah. So I think that's the, from our perspective, that's the 10,000 foot view. Yeah, and I think the questions we've answered in the module, we can reiterate here a few of them because we know they're the questions that people are asking. Like how long should it be and what should I write about? And those questions, there is no definitive answer to those questions, there is no template to follow because every market's going to be different, every relationship with every audience is going to be different. But the name really says it all, it is a value newsletter and the value that it creates is both for the audience primarily, but also for you as the business owner because it's that dual meaning. And for the audience, that's where you're focusing your attention. What is something I could share with my audience this week, this month, whatever your frequency is, that would be valuable to them? That's the starting point. And that will inform, and sometimes value is an in-depth article about a particular topic in a way that's digestible that somebody can go and use with a few action steps. We've certainly done that plenty of times. Other times value is more sort of light infotainment. We've done that as well, which is here are some things that we've been reading or listening to or some things we're interested in and not quite as cognitively heavily or intellectually demanding, but still useful, still valuable. And that's the starting point. What would be valuable to my audience in this period of time that's digestible, that's useful? And then of course, the second meaning of value newsletter is that it's a privilege, it's an opportunity to be in somebody's inbox where they're paying attention. And that's valuable to you as the business owner. So value newsletter, really think about those two, the two sides of that coin. But when it comes to thinking about how often, how long, what do I create, the emphasis really is on what is interesting value, informative, insightful for my audience right now in whatever context is relevant for you. All right, Andre, I think we've covered a lot of ground here. Anything else to add before we wrap up for the day? Yeah, I think that's been a nice compact 20 minutes or so. Yeah, this is good stuff. Perfect, and we'll just end the way we end just about everything else, which is get back to work. Get back to it.
